headlineSummary: HN sentiment is mostly neutral overall, with OpenAI showing mixed reactions from both praise and criticism [E2][E3][E4][E5], while Anthropic, Google Gemini, and Microsoft Copilot sit in a close tie with limited evidence and mostly mixed or negative comments [E1][E3][E4][E6].

ranking.summary: Google Gemini appears neutral overall but with limited evidence, and the main comment is negative about its reluctance to search the web; it is also part of a close tie with Anthropic and Microsoft Copilot [E3].
Microsoft Copilot is neutral overall with limited evidence, based on a negative coding-assistant anecdote, and it is in a close tie with Anthropic and Google Gemini [E6].

Anthropic lands in mixed neutral territory: one comment criticizes Claude’s reliability, while another says Claude has better UI and answers, and it is in a close tie with Google Gemini and Microsoft Copilot [E1][E4].
OpenAI is mixed neutral but draws the most discussion, with comments praising its research and guidance while others criticize silent downgrades and weaker instruction following [E2][E3][E4][E5].

evidence.excerpt: I am using API mode, and it's clear that there are times when the Claude model just gives up. And it is very noticeable because the model just does the most dumb things possible. "You have a bug in line 23." "Oh yes, this solution is bugged, let me delete the whole feature." That one-line fix I could make even with ChatGPT 3.5 can't just happen. Workflows that I use and are very reproducible start to flake and then fail. After a certain number of tokens per day, it becomes unusable. I like Claude, but I don't understand why they would do this.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly names Claude and strongly criticizes its reliability and usefulness, calling it unusable after a token threshold.

evidence.excerpt: It's even worse with ChatGPT and the like. E.g. I had to learn the hard way that at >45k input tokens ChatGPT 5.2 Thinking Extended bumps its intelligence down so hard that it can't follow basic instructions (or it somehow truncates the input, losing the instructions). It sucks to lose confidence in an otherwise great tool. I would 100x prefer being forced to back-off, or getting a straight-no, than getting silently downgraded. Transparency is a big deal.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly criticizes ChatGPT for silently degrading capability on long inputs and frames this as a transparency and trust problem.

evidence.excerpt: Also, it seems like Gemini is really averse to googling (which is ironic by itself) and ChatGPT, at least in the Thinking modes loves to look up current and correct info. If I ask something a bit more involved in Extended Thinking mode, it will think for several minutes and look up more than 100 sources. It's really good, practically a Deep Research inside of a normal chat.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly says Gemini is 'really averse to googling,' a negative comparison against ChatGPT's search behavior.
The commenter explicitly praises ChatGPT Thinking modes for looking up many sources and calls the result 'really good' and like Deep Research.

evidence.excerpt: ChatGPT 5.2 has been a good motivator for me to try out other LLMs because of how bad it is. Both 5.1 and 5.2 have been downgrades in terms of instruction following and accuracy, but 5.2 especially so. The upside is that that's had me using Claude much more, and I like a lot of things about it, both in terms of UI and the answers.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly calls ChatGPT 5.2 'bad' and says recent versions are downgrades in instruction following and accuracy.
The commenter explicitly says they are using Claude more and like many things about it, including the UI and answers.

evidence.excerpt: ChatGPT took the time to ask me questions and based on my answers, narrowed down a particularly scary diagnosis and gave excellent instructions on how to get to a local hospital in a foreign country, what to ask for, and that I didn't have to worry very much because it sounded very typical for what I had. The level of reassurance that I was doing everything right actually made me feel less scared, because it was a pretty serious problem. Everything it told me was 100% correct and it guided me perfectly.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly credits ChatGPT with accurate guidance, excellent instructions, and perfect guidance in a medical situation.

evidence.excerpt: Today I asked copilot agent a question about a selector in a cypress test and it requested to run a python command in my terminal.

annotations.rationale: In coding-assistant context, 'copilot agent' most likely refers to Microsoft/GitHub Copilot, and the commenter describes an obviously unhelpful suggestion.
